citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
Upon this focused inspection, when verifying the brown and white rabbit identified yesterday as shivering was no longer
cold after another night of freezing temperatures, the same rabbit was now bleeding from the base of its left ear and
appeared lethargic. When the inspector asked how the rabbit was doing before we arrived at the enclosure, the licensee
stated the rabbit was fine. The rabbit was no longer shivering but was found in a tucked body posture with its eyes mostly
closed, appearing sunken, with some cream-colored eye discharge accumulating in the corners. Fresh food had just been
offered, and this rabbit was not interested and stayed off by himself. Even when touched or lifted up, he returned to being
tucked and didn’t move away. Per the licensee, when asked further about this rabbit, he said for the past month this rabbit
has been off by himself. When specifically asked why he was bleeding, the licensee said the rabbits routinely fight and
cause wounds since all ten rabbits in this enclosure are males.
Failure to identify such conditions and to communicate findings with the veterinarian, delays treatment and causes undue
animal pain and distress. A more rigorous method of daily observation of the rabbits must be implemented to properly
assess their health and well-being. Direct and frequent communication with the attending veterinarian is required for
timely diagnosis and adequate medical attention. The currently affected rabbit needs to be seen by a veterinarian for an
appropriate diagnosis and treatment plan.
Correct by end of the day March 18, 2026.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the licensee.
n

narrative:
2.40(b)(3)  Direct
   
Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors). 
Upon this focused inspection, when verifying the brown and white rabbit identified yesterday as shivering was no longer 
cold after another night of freezing temperatures, the same rabbit was now bleeding from the base of its left ear and 
appeared lethargic. When the inspector asked how the rabbit was doing before we arrived at the enclosure, the licensee 
stated the rabbit was fine. The rabbit was no longer shivering but was found in a tucked body posture with its eyes mostly 
closed, appearing sunken, with some cream-colored eye discharge accumulating in the corners. Fresh food had just been 
offered, and this rabbit was not interested and stayed off by himself. Even when touched or lifted up, he returned to being 
tucked and didn’t move away. Per the licensee, when asked further about this rabbit, he said for the past month this rabbit 
has been off by himself. When specifically asked why he was bleeding, the licensee said the rabbits routinely fight and 
cause wounds since all ten rabbits in this enclosure are males. 
 
Failure to identify such conditions and to communicate findings with the veterinarian, delays treatment and causes undue 
animal pain and distress. A more rigorous method of daily observation of the rabbits must be implemented to properly 
assess their health and well-being. Direct and frequent communication with the attending veterinarian is required for 
timely diagnosis and adequate medical attention. The currently affected rabbit needs to be seen by a veterinarian for an 
appropriate diagnosis and treatment plan. 
Correct by end of the day March 18, 2026. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the licensee.
